title: Lab 12.2: Edge Proposal
summary: The edge now exposes a structured proposal that names the desired change, its assumptions, and the constraints it cannot validate locally.
decision_question: How does the system describe intent before authority approves it?

surfaces.name: cloud catalog
queries: show where final approval can occur

proposal.summary: The planner proposes moving the routing step closer to the edge to reduce round-trip latency on repeated requests.
assumptions: the route hint can be computed from locally visible inputs
edge placement reduces latency for downstream steps
unresolved: whether the routing decision is allowed in the edge trust zone
whether the resulting placement still satisfies audit obligations

validation.summary: The proposal is visible and structured, but has not yet been evaluated against the authoritative constraint set.
authority_notes: the edge is now useful for reasoning, but not for approval

execution.summary: No runtime move occurs yet because the system now treats proposal and execution as separate stages.

trace.summary: The proposal exists, but the system still does not preserve a trace artifact beyond the current planning surface.

functions.planning: Planning now emits a structured proposal that can be inspected independently of execution.
functions.validation: Validation remains separate because the edge does not claim authority it does not have.
functions.execution: Execution is intentionally blocked until the proposal reaches the authoritative layer.

expectations: The important gain is not edge execution yet; it is explicit, machine-readable intent.
A system becomes more understandable when it admits what it knows and what it cannot yet justify.
runtime_decisions: the planner records unresolved trust and compliance questions instead of guessing them
the system keeps authority in the cloud while still making the proposal visible at the edge
